tlos: TLO 1. Describe the fundamental principles governing the rigid-body equations of motion for aircraft.
TLO 2. Explain the differences between body-axis, stability-axis, and wind-axis reference frames in aircraft dynamics.
TLO 3. Apply the six-degree-of-freedom equations of motion to determine aircraft translational and rotational motion.
TLO 4. Characterize the effects of simplifying assumptions on the nonlinear and linearized equations of motion.
TLO 5. Compute aircraft dynamic responses using numerical methods to solve the equations of motion.
TLO 6. Discuss modern methods of interpreting equations of motion, such as uncertainty quantification and sensitivity analysis.

events.description: The rigid body equations of motion for an aircraft are derived, and then trim solutions are presented. The equations are linearized in both state space as well as transfer function methods.
The rigid body equations of motion for an aircraft are derived, and then trim solutions are presented. The equations are linearized in both state space as well as transfer function methods.
The rigid body equations of motion for an aircraft are derived, and then trim solutions are presented. The equations are linearized in both state space as well as transfer function methods.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain concepts of aircraft static and dynamic stability
TLO 2. Collect data to determine aircraft stability characteristics
TLO 3. Analyze data from flight test to determine aircraft stability characteristics
TLO 4. Compare flight test data to Mil-Std-1797B.

events.description: The concepts of static and dynamic stability are applied to aircraft. Aircraft modes of motion are introduced. Stability derivatives and their influence are presented. Finally, MIL-STD 1797B guidelines are introduced and applied to the static and dynamic stability parameters to predict an aircraft's handling qualities or to assess a specification compliance. Concepts taught in this course are reinforced using a simulation laboratory exercise.

The concepts of static and dynamic stability are applied to aircraft. Aircraft modes of motion are introduced. Stability derivatives and their influence are presented. Finally, MIL-STD 1797B guidelines are introduced and applied to the static and dynamic stability parameters to predict an aircraft's handling qualities or to assess a specification compliance. Concepts taught in this course are reinforced using a simulation laboratory exercise.

The concepts of static and dynamic stability are applied to aircraft. Aircraft modes of motion are introduced. Stability derivatives and their influence are presented. Finally, MIL-STD 1797B guidelines are introduced and applied to the static and dynamic stability parameters to predict an aircraft's handling qualities or to assess a specification compliance. Concepts taught in this course are reinforced using a simulation laboratory exercise.

This event introduces several Flight Test Techniques (FTTs) used in flying qualities testing and then uses those FTTs to explore how changing stability derivatives of an aircraft changes its flying qualities.

These sorties explore the "s-plane" to further explore how FTTs are used to investigate an aircraft, and to identify and qualify modes of motion. They also associate how open loop characteristics influence closed loop flying qualities and introduce handling qualities evaluations and the use of the Cooper-Harper rating scale.

These sorties explore the "s-plane" to further explore how FTTs are used to investigate an aircraft, and to identify and qualify modes of motion. They also associate how open loop characteristics influence closed loop flying qualities and introduce handling qualities evaluations and the use of the Cooper-Harper rating scale.

These sorties explore the "s-plane" to further explore how FTTs are used to investigate an aircraft, and to identify and qualify modes of motion. They also associate how open loop characteristics influence closed loop flying qualities and introduce handling qualities evaluations and the use of the Cooper-Harper rating scale.

These sorties explore the "s-plane" to further explore how FTTs are used to investigate an aircraft, and to identify and qualify modes of motion. They also associate how open loop characteristics influence closed loop flying qualities and introduce handling qualities evaluations and the use of the Cooper-Harper rating scale.
This event introduces open loop flying qualities of an unpowered aircraft.
This event introduces the students to FTTs used to assess an aircraft's static stability and to assess against a military specification.
This event introduces the students to FTTs used to assess an aircraft's static stability and to assess against a military specification.

This event introduces students to FTTs used to assess a fighter type aircraft's dynamic stability flying qualities and shows the dependence of flying qualities on flight condition. The sortie also introduces the student team to control room and disciplined engineer operations and flying qualities telemetered parameters review and assessment while managing an ongoing airborne test. Finally, the event will introduce CR operations to CSO/P/RPA aircrew.

This event introduces students to FTTs used to assess a fighter type aircraft's dynamic stability flying qualities and shows the dependence of flying qualities on flight condition. The sortie also introduces the student team to control room and disciplined engineer operations and flying qualities telemetered parameters review and assessment while managing an ongoing airborne test. Finally, the event will introduce CR operations to CSO/P/RPA aircrew.

This event introduces students to FTTs used to assess a fighter type aircraft's dynamic stability flying qualities and shows the dependence of flying qualities on flight condition. The sortie also introduces the student team to control room and disciplined engineer operations and flying qualities telemetered parameters review and assessment while managing an ongoing airborne test. Finally, the event will introduce CR operations to CSO/P/RPA aircrew.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to evaluate an aircraft's unaugmented static stability flying qualities against those found in MIL-STD 1797B. The student will also be able to identify and use unaugmented dynamic stability criteria found in MIL-STD 1797B.

events.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Brief
events.description: This event introduces the students to the flying qualities T-38 test plan. It will introduce the plan in three parts. The first part is longitudinal where students are left with constructing cards from given test objectives and measures of performance. The second part is lateral-directional where students will develop measures of performance and cards from given objectives. The third part will be conducted later after handling qualities academics. In that phase students will have to determine objectives, measures of performance and develop cards from a set of requirements. In all parts, students will be responsible for defending their test approaches and mitigation/build-up plans to a reviewing T38 IP and IFTE.

events.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Data Flight 1
events.description: These are student designed and planned flights allowing the students to gather data in support of their flying qualities test plan report.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Dynamics Control Room (ABM/FTE with CSO/P/RPA desired as DE)
T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Working Group

events.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Data Flight 2
events.description: These are student designed and planned flights allowing the students to gather data in support of their flying qualities test plan report.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Dynamics Control Room (ABM/FTE with CSO/P/RPA desired as DE)
T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Working Group

events.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Working Group
events.description: This a project composes several events, all tightly integrated to guide the student along its progression. The student will explore MIL-STD-1797 while collecting data in an unsupervised fashion. It will give the student the opportunity to participate in planning a test across multiple flights while ensuring the quality of the data collected is high enough to write a report detailing the findings. The student will develop measures of performance and test maneuvers to assess the open loop flying qualities of the T-38 as an area defense fighter. Not only does this event illustrate how flying qualities or spec-compliance testing might happen, but it also enforces requirements traceability and associates test maneuvers with test objectives via measures of performance. All the student's testing experience to this point will be required to make a final recommendation as to whether to purchase this aircraft for a developing nation.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Brief
T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Data Flight 2

Not only does this event illustrate how flying qualities or spec-compliance testing might happen, but it also enforces requirements traceability and associates test maneuvers with test objectives via measures of performance. All the student's testing experience to this point will be required to make a final recommendation as to whether to purchase this aircraft for a developing nation.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Brief
T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Data Flight 2

tlos: TLO 1. The student will understand the basic "vocabulary" of flight control systems and will be conversant in that vocabulary in a technical flight test environment.
TLO 2. The student will demonstrate how to use Bode and root locus tools to evaluate the dynamics of a feedback control system.
TLO 3. The student will understand some of the compromises in flight control implementation of both continuous and discrete systems.

TLO 4. The student will demonstrate predicting flying qualities and apply the pitch and roll requirements of MIL-STD-1797B to identify differences between the military flying qualities and civilian airworthiness requirements. The student will understand how to recognize the characteristics of pilot-induced oscillations.

TLO 5. The student will understand some of the fundamental challenges in flight test development of automatic control systems. The student will be able to identify the types of models used in flight control system development and will understand how the elements of various models work together to affect realism. The student will be able to communicate the steps in the verification and validation process essential to flight control system development.

TLO 6. The student will understand the differences between classical and modern aircraft control design techniques. The student will be able to communicate the applicability of traditional analysis methods and design standards to modern control system design.

TLO 7. The student will understand the implications of outer-loop control as it applies to manned and unmanned air vehicles. The student will be able to describe the purpose of various outer-loop control implementations.
TLO 8. The student will understand the methods used to identify aircraft response dynamics from flight and ground testing.
TLO 9. The student will understand how the models of human operator dynamics impact design for flying qualities.
TLO 10. The student will be able understand how to apply concepts of solid mechanics and mechanics of materials to aircraft structure design and test.
TLO 11. The student will understand how to identify the regulations and standards appropriate for a given structure.

TLO 12. The student will understand the effects that non-rigid bodies have on aircraft flight characteristics including when to apply appropriate flight or ground testing techniques to investigate those effects.
TLO 13. The student will be familiar with the excitation and instrumentation systems required for various structural tests.
TLO 14. The student will be able to understand structural test data for use in verifying regulations and standards compliance for a given aircraft.
TLO 15. The student will be familiar with major safety concerns with aerospace systems structural test and use the buildup approach to safely test those systems.

events.description: Feedback flight control systems are introduced along with motivation for transition from mechanical to automatic flight control systems. Linear analysis techniques are presented with applications to aircraft flight controls. These fundamental concepts will be used throughout the 7000-level course. Feedback flight control system implementation is introduced to demonstrate how augmented control systems are used to enhance air vehicle stability and performance. Examples of classical proportional, integral, derivative and LQR (linear quadratic regulator) control systems are presented as is gain scheduling, mode shaping. Basic linear control design and analysis techniques are then applied to aircraft. Considerations for modern aircraft with relevant examples are given as well as issues associated with flight testing control systems. Finally, an introduction to digital control and other analysis planes are discussed.

Feedback flight control systems are introduced along with motivation for transition from mechanical to automatic flight control systems. Linear analysis techniques are presented with applications to aircraft flight controls. These fundamental concepts will be used throughout the 7000-level course.

Feedback flight control systems are introduced along with motivation for transition from mechanical to automatic flight control systems. Linear analysis techniques are presented with applications to aircraft flight controls.

tlos: TLO 1. Define relevant terms and concepts of solid mechanics as applied to aerospace structures
TLO 2. Identify safety concerns with structural test of aerospace systems
TLO 3. Justify when structural testing is required and the appropriate standards for a given structure
TLO 4. Assess appropriate test techniques and instrumentation for various types of structures test
TLO 5. Develop safe test planning by utilizing a buildup approach that addresses key structural concerns
TLO 6. Calculate basic quantities associated with structures testing such as stress, strain, and % Design Limit Load.
TLO 7. Appraise potential structural dynamics test safety concerns for a test of an aerospace system.
TLO 8. Identify when structural dynamics testing is required and the appropriate regulations and standards for a given structure.
TLO 9. Assess appropriate test techniques and instrumentation for various types of structural dynamics test.
TLO 10. Develop safe structural dynamics test planning by utilizing a buildup approach that addresses key structural concerns.
TLO 11. Determine/Estimate basic quantities associated with structural dynamics testing such as flutter speed, flutter margin, gain margin, phase margin.
TLO 12. Justify the role of the different components of the flutter Analyze-Test-Correlate airworthiness process.

events.description: Static structural analysis is presented along with basic stress/strain analysis. Dynamics applied to non-rigid bodies is also presented, to include basic dynamics and ending with flutter and aeroservoelasticity. A fundamental exposition of basic analysis techniques used for aircraft structures is reviewed. Statics and dynamic structural test. Techniques are presented and include both ground and flight test considerations and buildup.

Static structural analysis is presented along with basic stress/strain analysis. Dynamics applied to non-rigid bodies is also presented, to include basic dynamics and ending with flutter and aeroservoelasticity. A fundamental exposition of basic analysis techniques used for aircraft structures is reviewed. Statics and dynamic structural test. Techniques are presented and include both ground and flight test considerations and buildup.

Static structural analysis is presented along with basic stress/strain analysis. Dynamics applied to non-rigid bodies is also presented, to include basic dynamics and ending with flutter and aeroservoelasticity.

Static structural analysis is presented along with basic stress/strain analysis. Dynamics applied to non-rigid bodies is also presented, to include basic dynamics and ending with flutter and aeroservoelasticity.

events.eventName: Flight Control Systems Development and Integration Testing Lecture
events.description: The testing process used to clear flight control algorithms for flight test is covered. Flight simulation facilities are discussed. The processes for ground test verification, structural coupling testing, and rigid body limit cycle testing are also discussed.

events.eventName: Learjet Flight Control Systems Verification Ground Test MIB
events.description: This event explores how flight control system verification and validation is executed including an introduction to ground vibration testing and a demonstration of how structural notch filters are implemented.

prerequisites.name: Flight Control Systems Development and Integration Testing Lecture

events.eventName: Learjet Flight Control Systems Verification Ground Test
events.description: This event explores how flight control system verification and validation is executed including an introduction to ground vibration testing and a demonstration of how structural notch filters are implemented.

prerequisites.name: Flight Control Systems Development and Integration Testing Lecture

events.description: This flight is the students' first look at how a structural envelope expansion test might be constructed and executed, placing emphasis on safe envelope expansion techniques and proper realization of and reactions to abort criteria for elevated risk flight test.

events.eventName: F-16 Structures Envelope Expansion Control Room
events.description: This flight is the students' first look at how a structural envelope expansion test might be constructed and executed, placing emphasis on safe envelope expansion techniques and proper realization of and reactions to abort criteria for elevated risk flight test.

This flight is the students' first look at how a structural envelope expansion test might be constructed and executed, placing emphasis on safe envelope expansion techniques and proper realization of and reactions to abort criteria for elevated risk flight test.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand the use of different control methods and their applications.
TLO 2. Understand the use of different simulations and their applications.
TLO 3. Understand different data collection methods and their applications.
TLO 4. Evaluate the "sim to real gap" for a given scenario.
TLO 5. Create hazard and risk mitigation strategies for various autonomy scenarios.
TLO 6. Apply control methods to different applications.
TLO 7. Analyze robustness methods and training tools for their accuracy and proper information.
TLO 8. Analyze methods for testing autonomous systems.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences
events.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It includes a broad scope of topics that build an understanding of aspects to consider when testing an autonomy-based system. Topics include identifying and analyzing different types of control systems and when to apply them, describing different categories of data and proper methods for their analysis, describing different forms and fidelity of simulation and their application, analyzing the use of Monte Carlo testing to include its advantages and pitfalls, and identifying key issues to consider when building up to and executing flight tests.
This block will be the capstone for Data-Driven Control. It involves a hands-on exercise applying the skills learned throughout the program related to data-driven test.

prerequisites.name: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences

tlos: TLO 1. The student will understand the basic "vocabulary" of handling qualities testing and will be conversant in that vocabulary in a technical flight test environment.

TLO 2. The student will be able to evaluate an aircraft using the Cooper-Harper rating scale and be able to provide proper pilot opinion to support those ratings. In giving and recording pilot opinion, the student will apply the concept that pilot dynamics are coupled with flying qualities to create handling qualities.
TLO 3. The student will be able to evaluate an aircraft for PIO tendencies.
TLO 4. The student will be able to understand the differences of handling qualities of piloted and unpiloted aircraft.
TLO 5. The student will be able to evaluate the dynamics of an aircraft based on MIL-STD-1797B.

events.description: The Cooper Harper rating scale is introduced. PIO rating scales are also introduced. A human's effect on aircraft stability is discussed. The different phases of handling qualities testing are presented. Concepts taught in this course are reinforced using a simulation laboratory exercise.

prerequisites.name: Flight Control Systems Development and Integration Testing Lecture

events.description: The Cooper Harper rating scale is introduced. PIO rating scales are also introduced. A human's effect on aircraft stability is discussed. The different phases of handling qualities testing are presented.

events.description: These simulator sessions provide a basis for how handling qualities ratings are given and explores the details of using the Cooper-Harper Ratings Scale and the PIO Ratings Scale as well as the importance of pilot comments when evaluating the handling qualities of an aircraft. The use of numerical methods to analyze multiple ratings from different pilots will be discussed.

These simulator sessions provide a basis for how handling qualities ratings are given and explores the details of using the Cooper-Harper Ratings Scale and the PIO Ratings Scale as well as the importance of pilot comments when evaluating the handling qualities of an aircraft. The use of numerical methods to analyze multiple ratings from different pilots will be discussed.

events.eventName: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report
events.description: This project is a continuation of the FQ 6301A Flying Qualities Test Plan Project. Building on the knowledge the student gained from that project, they will first design handling qualities tasks to evaluate the mission performance of the T-38 as an area defense fighter for foreign military sales. The student will be briefed in the FQ 8201M T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan MIB where expectations will be set and requirements for the project will be discussed. Next they will design tasks and present test cards to be signed by a T-38 Instructor Pilot. The student will then go execute their plan to gather data in FQ 8202F T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Data Flight. Once you have collected and analyzed all your data, you will present your findings to a board in the FQ 8210Y T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report.

events.description: Various vehicle failure modes are evaluated in this block. Specifically, engine out testing is addressed. Failure mode and effect testing/analysis are addressed.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe the mechanics and contributors to aircraft stalls.
TLO 2. Describe the mechanics and dangers of inertial and kinematic coupling.
TLO 3. Describe the mechanics of spins and the aircraft properties that contribute to them.
TLO 4. Determine how to recover from spins in various aircraft types.
TLO 5. Explain how to conduct stall, departure, and spin testing.

events.eventName: Stall, Departure and Spin Testing Lecture
events.description: The aerodynamics associated with stall are presented and various high-angle-of-attack terms are formally defined. The equations of motion are revisited for coupling and spins and then analyzed. High angle-of-attack test methodologies are presented including those discussed by MIL-STD-1797B and MIL-F-83691B. Analysis and recovery techniques for this regime of flight are developed for use in future flight tests.

events.eventName: Stall, Departure and Spin Testing Exam
events.description: The aerodynamics associated with stall are presented and various high-angle-of-attack terms are formally defined. The equations of motion are revisited for coupling and spins and then analyzed.

events.eventName: Stall, Departure and Spin Testing Debrief
events.description: The aerodynamics associated with stall are presented and various high-angle-of-attack terms are formally defined.
This flight puts into practice buildup concepts and FTTs commonly used in aircraft spin testing.

events.eventName: F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion MIB
events.description: For the High AOA Envelope Expansion project, the student will receive project requirements and be given information needed to complete the project the MIB. The block will then be given time to develop a plan during the instructor facilitated FQ 9231W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 1. The block will then be broken up into teams where they will finalize their plan during FQ 9232W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 2 and FQ9233W High AOA Envelope Expansion FRR. The students will demonstrate to their instructors that they can execute their plan in the rehearsal sim and control room. Finally, the teams will execute their plan on the data flight and control room. The team will analyze and report on their data as part of FQ 9238Y F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion Oral Report.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will recall the process of certifying aircraft stores.

events.description: The analytical process and flight test methods used to qualify stores on aircraft for safe operation are introduced.

tlos: TLO 1. Describe how the concept of build-up approach is applied to first flights and envelope expansion testing.
TLO 2. Describe how risk mitigation is addressed in test planning and mission materials in a first flight and in envelope expansion.
TLO 3. Describe the types of testing that are done during ground testing, low-speed taxi, and high-speed taxi.
TLO 4. Describe how the elements comprising a maneuver block are determined.
TLO 5. Given an FTT, describe what information is provided and which test objectives can be met.
TLO 6. Describe the general flight conditions and configurations that should be tested during a first flight.
TLO 7. List the major components of a generic first flight profile.

events.description: This block introduces the concept of aircraft flight envelope expansion as well as the creation of aircraft flight manuals using testing that will occur during the Flying Qualities Phase.

events.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Lecture
events.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

events.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Exam
events.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

events.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Debrief
events.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will execute a flying qualities test.

events.eventName: Sim Flying Qualities Practical Exam MIB
events.description: The objective of the sim is to evaluate the student ABM/CSO/RPA ability to plan, execute, and report a flying and handling qualities evaluation using hand-held data techniques. The students are given a scenario in which they must conduct an initial evaluation of a proposed solution to address the significant time delays often encountered when flying RPAs or handling remotely guided munitions. The student is tasked to plan and fly the sim then provide an informal written report on the results and recommendations.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report

events.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Practical Exam MIB
events.description: The objective of the sortie is to evaluate the student pilot/FTE ability to plan, execute, and report a flying and handling qualities evaluation. The students are given a scenario in which information on the T-38 pitch PIO susceptibility at high speed and low altitude is requested. It will take more than one sortie to provide this information, so an engineering team elects to fly a single sortie to narrow the focus of the effort. The students are tasked to plan and fly this sortie then provide an informal written report to the engineering team on the results and recommendations for worthwhile additional testing.
